title: Ramesh Kumar Verma v. Hindustan Steel Components Pvt. Ltd. & Anr.
court: High Court of Judicature at Allahabad
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without following the mandatory principles of natural justice, as no proper show cause notice was issued and no departmental enquiry was conducted in accordance with the Standing Orders.

The respondent company failed to produce any valid evidence of misconduct attributed to the petitioner, and the chargesheet was vague and lacked specificity as required under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946.

The petitioner had served the respondent company for over fourteen years without any prior adverse remark on his service record, and the sudden termination was actuated by malafide intention due to his active participation in trade union activities.

The Labour Court had already adjudicated in favour of the petitioner under Section 10 of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, and the respondent's writ petition challenging that award deserved to be dismissed.
The termination violated Section 25-F of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, as no retrenchment compensation or one month's notice pay was provided to the petitioner prior to termination.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Allahabad upheld the award of the Labour Court in favour of the petitioner-workman Ramesh Kumar Verma, finding that his termination by Hindustan Steel Components Pvt. Ltd. was illegal, arbitrary, and in violation of principles of natural justice and the provisions of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947. The court directed reinstatement of the petitioner with full back wages and continuity of service from the date of his illegal termination.
judgmentText:
JUDGMENT

Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

Hon'ble Mr. Justice Sudhir Narain Pandey

Heard learned counsel for both the parties and perused the record.

1. This writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution of India has been filed by the respondent-management, Hindustan Steel Components Pvt. Ltd., challenging the award dated 22nd September, 2021 passed by the Presiding Officer, Labour Court No. III, Kanpur, in Adjudication Case No. 47 of 2019, whereby the Labour Court held the termination of the workman, Ramesh Kumar Verma, to be illegal and directed his reinstatement with full back wages.

2. The brief facts, as emerging from the record, are that the workman-petitioner was employed as a Senior Machine Operator with the respondent-management since the year 2007. On 18th March, 2019, the management issued a chargesheet alleging that the petitioner had engaged in acts of insubordination and wilful negligence causing loss to the company. The petitioner submitted his reply to the chargesheet on 3rd April, 2019. Thereafter, without conducting any proper domestic enquiry, the management proceeded to terminate the services of the petitioner vide order dated 15th June, 2019.

3. This Court has carefully examined the award of the Labour Court and finds no infirmity or perversity warranting interference under Article 226 of the Constitution. The Labour Court rightly observed that the enquiry officer appointed by the management did not afford adequate opportunity to the workman to cross-examine the witnesses produced against him. The enquiry proceedings were concluded in a mechanical and perfunctory manner. The principles of natural justice are not mere technical requirements but are the soul of fair adjudication, as held by the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Workmen of Firestone Tyre and Rubber Co. v. Management, AIR 1973 SC 1227.

4. This Court further notes that the management has failed to demonstrate any specific act of misconduct with cogent documentary or oral evidence. The chargesheet itself was vague and omnibus in nature. A mere allegation of misconduct, unsupported by evidence, cannot sustain an order of termination, particularly against a workman of fourteen years standing with an unblemished service record.

5. It is also evident from the record that the petitioner was an active member of the registered trade union operating within the establishment, and the timing of the termination, coinciding with the period of union wage revision negotiations, raises a strong inference of victimisation. The management has offered no plausible explanation to dispel this inference.

6. The contention of the management that back wages should not be granted in full is rejected. The petitioner remained unemployed during the intervening period and has placed on record a sworn affidavit to this effect, which has not been rebutted. In view of the settled legal position enunciated in Deepali Gundu Surwase v. Kranti Junior Adhyapak Mahavidyalaya, (2013) 10 SCC 324, the workman is entitled to full back wages unless the employer proves gainful employment elsewhere.

7. For all the foregoing reasons, the writ petition filed by the management is hereby dismissed. The award of the Labour Court dated 22nd September, 2021 is confirmed. The respondent-management is directed to reinstate the petitioner-workman with full back wages and continuity of service within a period of sixty days from the date of this judgment.

8. There shall be no order as to costs.

(Justice Sudhir Narain Pandey)
High Court of Judicature at Allahabad
14th March, 2023
